unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8. This unit is called "Media, Technology, and News" because you will explore how information travels in the modern world and why it matters to think critically about everything you read, watch, and share. Every day, millions of messages flow through newspapers, television channels, websites, and social media platforms. Some of these messages are carefully researched and truthful. Others are misleading, exaggerated, or completely false. As a young person growing up in the digital age, one of the most important skills you can develop is the ability to tell the difference between reliable information and misinformation.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners verifying media, technology and news sources

The word express belongs to our communication and media vocabulary.
I can explain express in my own words.
I can connect express to this unit's texts.
I can use express in a clear Grade 7 sentence.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'express', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Media, Technology, and News.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain what media literacy means and why it is important for young people today.
Identify the key features of a reliable news source and an unreliable one.
Read a text about media and answer comprehension and analysis questions in full sentences.
Use reported speech correctly to report what someone said or wrote.
Recognise and use common multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) related to media and communication.
Form and use first and second conditional sentences to discuss possibilities and consequences.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write a school magazine article that follows journalistic conventions such as headlines, leads, and quotes.

readings.title: Media, Technology, and News: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 8. This unit is called "Media, Technology, and News" because you will explore how information travels in the modern world and why it matters to think critically about everything you read, watch, and share. Every day, millions of messages flow through newspapers, television channels, websites, and social media platforms. Some of these messages are carefully researched and truthful. Others are misleading, exaggerated, or completely false. As a young person growing up in the digital age, one of the most important skills you can develop is the ability to tell the difference between reliable information and misinformation.
Throughout this unit you will read a new passage about a school journalism club that investigates a viral rumour, study the grammar of reported speech, multi-word verbs, and conditionals, and complete a writing workshop where you create a school magazine article and analyse fake news. You will also take part in speaking and listening activities that bring media literacy to life, including role-play interviews, news broadcasts, and structured debates. By the end of this unit you will understand how news is created, how to evaluate sources, and how to communicate information responsibly and accurately. These are skills that will serve you well in every subject, in your community, and in your future career.
In Islam, truthfulness is one of the highest virtues. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said, "Truthfulness leads to righteousness, and righteousness leads to Paradise." The Quran also warns us in Surah Al-Hujurat (49:6) to verify information before acting upon it: "O you who believe, if a sinful person brings you news, verify it, lest you harm people unknowingly and then regret what you have done." This unit will help you put that principle into practice every time you encounter a piece of news or a claim on social media.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what media literacy means and why it is important for young people today.
Identify the key features of a reliable news source and an unreliable one.
Read a text about media and answer comprehension and analysis questions in full sentences.
Use reported speech correctly to report what someone said or wrote.
Recognise and use common multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) related to media and communication.
Form and use first and second conditional sentences to discuss possibilities and consequences.
Write a school magazine article that follows journalistic conventions such as headlines, leads, and quotes.
Analyse a piece of fake news and explain why it is unreliable, using specific evidence.
Take part in a mock news broadcast, a debate about digital media ethics, and a role-play interview.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment statements and set goals for improvement.
The words below appear throughout this unit. Read each definition carefully and study the example sentence. Try to use at least four of these words in your own writing this week. Whenever you meet an unfamiliar word in the reading passage or activities, come back to this table. Your full vocabulary companion file has all thirty unit words with extended practice activities.
Reading: Breaking News at Ilays Academy
Read the following passage carefully. It tells the story of a school journalism club that investigates a viral rumour. As you read, pay attention to the steps the students take to verify the information and how they communicate their findings. Think about what you would do if you encountered a similar situation at your own school.

readings.title: Media, Technology, and News: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was a Tuesday morning when Ishmael first saw the message on his phone. Someone had posted a video on social media claiming that Ilays Academy would be shut down at the end of the month. The video showed a blurry photograph of a letter supposedly from the Ministry of Education, and a voice said, "This school has failed its inspection. All students must find new schools immediately."
Within minutes, the message had been shared hundreds of times. Parents began calling the school in a panic. Some students arrived at the gates looking worried, asking their friends if the news was true. Ishmael's younger sister Sophia ran to him during the morning break, her eyes wide with fear.
"Is it true?" she asked. "Are they closing our school?"
Ishmael looked at the video again carefully. Something about it did not feel right. The letter in the photograph was blurry, and he could not see an official stamp or signature. The voice in the video belonged to someone he did not recognise, and there was no name or source given. He remembered what his English teacher, Ustadh Musa, had taught them about evaluating sources: always check who is making the claim, look for evidence, and verify information before sharing it.
"I do not think this is real," Ishmael told Sophia. "But let us find out for certain."
During the lunch break, Ishmael gathered a small group of students from the school's journalism club. They decided to investigate the rumour systematically. First, they visited the school office and asked the administrator directly. The administrator confirmed that no such letter existed and that the school had passed its most recent inspection with excellent marks. She even showed them the real inspection report.
Next, Yusra, the club's best researcher, searched the Ministry of Education's official website. There was no announcement about closing any school in their district. She also checked the accounts that had shared the original video and noticed they were all very new accounts with no other posts, which is a common sign of a deliberate misinformation campaign.
The journalism club wrote a short, clear article for the school noticeboard and the school's messaging group. They included three pieces of evidence: a quote from the administrator, a link to the official inspection results, and an explanation of why the video was not trustworthy. They also added a helpful list of tips for spotting fake news, such as checking the source, looking for official evidence, and waiting before sharing unverified information.
By the end of the day, the panic had calmed. Parents who had been worried sent messages of thanks. The headteacher praised the journalism club in the next assembly and said, "These students have shown us that the best response to false information is not fear or anger. It is careful investigation, clear communication, and the truth."
Ishmael felt proud. He had learned something important that day: in the age of social media, everyone has a responsibility to check information before they believe it or share it. The truth does not spread by itself. It needs people who care enough to find it and share it responsibly.
Now that you have read the passage about Ilays Academy, answer the following questions in full sentences. Write your answers in your exercise book or type them on your device. Think carefully about the details in the text and use evidence from the passage to support your answers.

readings.title: The Duty of Truth, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The first day back after the mid-term break started with a murmur in the halls of Bahari Secondary School. Everyone was talking about the new rule that had been posted abruptly on the school notice board: "All Form 3 students must now report for Saturday cleaning duty. Attendance is mandatory."
Sarah stood reading the notice, arms folded. She was the liaison between the student council and the school board — a role that required calm thinking and strong communication.
"They've added a new duty without asking anyone," she muttered. "No explanation. No vote."
"I heard it was the deputy head teacher's idea," said Isaac, her classmate. "Apparently he wants to punish us for being 'lazy' during the last clean-up day."
"But that's not true," Sarah said. "Our class actually contributed the most."
During lunch, the student council held an emergency meeting. As they gathered under the neem tree, Sarah pulled out her notebook.
"We need to respond," she said. "Either we accept this quietly, or we challenge it the right way."
Their secretary, Sophia, raised her hand. "Let's write a formal letter requesting an amendment to the rule."
"But who will sign it?" asked Isaac.
"Everyone," said Sarah. "If we want to be taken seriously."
They agreed to organize a petition. Meanwhile, Sarah offered to consult the deputy head teacher for more explanation.
She visited his office that afternoon.
"We've just received complaints from parents," he said. "So we added the duty to teach accountability."
"Sir, the students weren't even informed. The rule appeared overnight."
"The board agreed. It is a necessary policy."
"But it feels more like punishment than learning."
He leaned back. "It's not up for debate."
Back outside, Sarah sighed. She was beginning to realize that being a delegate wasn't just about passing messages — it was about standing firm.
That evening, she and Sophia drafted a letter:
To the School Board: We request an amendment to the newly posted Saturday rule. We feel the decision was made without student input. We suggest an alternate plan where duty is shared across all forms, or rotated biweekly.
They included a list of students who had contributed to the last clean-up. It was clear: Form 3 had not been negligent.
By Friday, over 200 students had signed the petition. It was submitted to the principal's office.
That weekend, instead of cleaning, the students held a quiet assembly in the school field, holding signs that read "Shared Duty is Fair Duty."
The following Monday, a notice appeared: The Saturday duty rule has been paused. A review is underway.
But the story didn't end there.
The school board called for a student representative to attend their monthly meeting. Sarah was chosen.
She entered the conference room where teachers, community leaders, and government officials sat. The atmosphere was formal. She was nervous, but ready.
A senior board member asked, "Miss Sarah, why are students opposed to the cleaning rule?"
She stood and replied, "We are not opposed to cleaning. We are opposed to unfair allocation of tasks. Either all students share the duty, or it becomes a punishment. That is unjust."
Another member raised a paper. "But an anonymous teacher alleges that students in Form 3 have skipped duty before."
"That's a claim," Sarah said. "But we provided logs showing our participation."

readings.title: The Duty of Truth, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The principal cleared his throat. "Actually, the student records indicate that Form 3 cleaned more consecutively than other forms."
"Well," one board member said, "this is a good example of democratic dialogue."
A proposal was made to delegate cleaning duties in a rotating system. Sarah helped draft the new schedule.
Later that day, she met with her classmates.
"We did it. The rule has been changed."
"You were amazing," said Sophia. "Your voice really influenced them."
Sarah shrugged. "I think the facts did. I just helped present them."
The news spread fast. Students felt more eligible to speak up now. Others who had felt ignored started joining meetings. The school felt more united.
The English teacher, Mr. Ibrahim, was especially proud.
He said, "Sarah has shown us the power of communication. This is not just about a rule — it's about how rules are made."
As part of an assignment, Sarah wrote a report titled Civic Responsibility in School Spaces. In it, she explained how one small act can lead to big development.
She concluded: "It is important to question rules that are imposed without consulting those they affect. Students are not just followers. They are also citizens."
Meanwhile, another issue came up. A student had been accused of vandalizing a locker. But no one saw it happen.
"He's liable," said one prefect. "He was nearby."
"But that's not proof," Sarah said. "You can't punish someone on what's alleged."
They held a hearing. After testimonies were reviewed, the boy was exonerated. It turned out a loose screw had caused the damage.
"From now on," said Sarah, "let's rely on facts, not fear."
By the end of term, the student council had created a newsletter. They corresponded regularly with the board. More students became industrious, organizing events, tutoring, and contributing to school improvement.
The school even tried a hybrid student-teacher planning group to create future policies.
And as Sarah looked over the minutes of their last meeting, she smiled.
Progress isn't just about big speeches. It's about organizing voices, balancing rights and rules, and believing that structure can serve justice.
Even when it starts with a mop and a notice board.

grammar.title: Present Perfect + Just
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense is used to talk about actions or experiences that happened at an unspecified time in the past but are important now. When we add just, we emphasise that something happened very recently. This is especially useful in news reporting, where journalists often say things like "The government has just announced a new policy" to show that the news is fresh and current. Understanding this structure will help you share news and describe recent events more clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present perfect tense is used to talk about actions or experiences that happened at an unspecified time in the past but are important now. When we add just, we emphasise that something happened very recently. This is especially useful in news reporting, where journalists often say things like "The government has just announced a new policy" to show that the news is fresh and current. Understanding this structure will help you share news and describe recent events more clearly.
How to form it
Structure: Subject + have/has + just + past participle
For I, you, we, they: use have. Example: "They have just completed the project."
For he, she, it: use has. Example: "She has just finished her homework."
Negative form: Add not after have/has.
"He has not just arrived."  OR  "He hasn't just arrived."
Question form: Switch the order of the subject and have/has.
"Have you just eaten?"
"Has she just called?"
Real sentence examples
"They have just completed the project."
"He has just called his friend."
"We have just seen that movie."
"She has just been exonerated."
"The journalist has just published a breaking news article."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Using the wrong form of "have".
Incorrect: "He have just finished the game."
Correct: "He has just finished the game."
Mistake 2: Mixing up the past participle.
Incorrect: "They have just go to the store."
Correct: "They have just gone to the store."
Memory tip
Think of just as a little alarm bell that tells the listener: "This happened moments ago! Pay attention!" Just always sits right before the past participle.
Practice
Fill in the blanks with the correct form of the verb and the word "just":
"I __________ (see) the new playground."
"They __________ (finish) their lunch."
"She __________ (receive) a letter."
"The reporter __________ (publish) the article."
Answers: 1. have just seen  2. have just finished  3. has just received  4. has just published
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply present perfect + just in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Connectives
grammar.explanation:
What they are and why they matter
Connectives are words that help us link ideas together in sentences. They make our writing and speaking clearer and more organised. By using connectives, we can show how one idea relates to another, such as cause and effect, contrast, or purpose. In media writing, connectives are essential for building logical arguments and explaining complex events clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are and why they matter
Connectives are words that help us link ideas together in sentences. They make our writing and speaking clearer and more organised. By using connectives, we can show how one idea relates to another, such as cause and effect, contrast, or purpose. In media writing, connectives are essential for building logical arguments and explaining complex events clearly.
How to use them
Here are four important connectives and their uses:
Because — to show a reason: "I wore my raincoat because it was raining."
So that — to express purpose: "I studied hard so that I could pass the test."
Therefore — to indicate a conclusion: "It was very cold; therefore, we stayed inside."
Although — to show contrast: "Although it was raining, we went for a walk."
Common mistakes
Confusing because (reason) with so that (purpose). "I studied because I could pass" should be "I studied so that I could pass."
Forgetting the comma after a connective at the start of a sentence: "Although it was late, they decided to finish."
Practice
Fill in the blanks with the correct connective: because, so that, therefore, or although.
I wanted to go outside, __________ it was raining.
She studied hard, __________ she could get good grades.
It was a sunny day; __________, we decided to have a picnic.
The cat was hungry __________ it meowed loudly.
Answers: 1. although  2. so that  3. therefore  4. because
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply connectives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Both, Either, and Neither
grammar.explanation:
What they mean
These three words help us talk about groups of two things or people. They are very useful in discussions and debates where you need to compare options or show agreement and disagreement.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they mean
These three words help us talk about groups of two things or people. They are very useful in discussions and debates where you need to compare options or show agreement and disagreement.
Both — refers to two things together: "Both newspapers reported the story."
Either — refers to one of two options: "You can read either the online version or the print version."
Neither — refers to not one or the other: "Neither the headline nor the article was accurate."
Key grammar rules
Both is always used with plural nouns and verbs: "Both reports are accurate."
Either and neither are used with singular nouns when referring to one of two: "Either option is fine."
Neither takes nor (not "or"): "Neither the reporter nor the editor checked the facts."
Common mistakes
Incorrect: "You can have both ice cream or cake." Correct: "You can have either ice cream or cake."
Incorrect: "Neither the cats or the dogs are here." Correct: "Neither the cats nor the dogs are here."
Practice
Choose the correct word (both, either, neither) to fill in the blanks:
You can choose __________ the red pen or the blue pen.
__________ my mum and dad are coming to the meeting.
__________ the headline nor the article was accurate.
__________ versions of the story are interesting to read.
Answers: 1. either  2. Both  3. Neither  4. Both
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply both, either, and neither in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: The Past Continuous Tense
grammar.explanation:
What it means
The past continuous tense describes actions that were happening at a specific moment in the past. It helps us set the scene and explain what was going on when something else occurred. In news writing, it is used to describe background actions: "The students were discussing the article when the bell rang."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means
The past continuous tense describes actions that were happening at a specific moment in the past. It helps us set the scene and explain what was going on when something else occurred. In news writing, it is used to describe background actions: "The students were discussing the article when the bell rang."
How to form it
Structure: Subject + was/were + verb(-ing)
Use was with singular subjects (I, he, she, it): "She was reading the newspaper."
Use were with plural subjects (you, we, they): "They were consulting their lawyer."
Real examples
"They were consulting their lawyer when they received the bad news."
"While I was studying, my brother was playing video games."
"She was reading a book while waiting for her friend."
"The reporter was writing the article when the new information arrived."
Common mistakes
Incorrect: "They was playing soccer." Correct: "They were playing soccer."
Incorrect: "She was read a book." Correct: "She was reading a book."
Practice
Complete the sentences using the past continuous tense:
While I __________ (watch) TV, my mum __________ (cook) dinner.
They __________ (play) soccer when it __________ (start) to rain.
She __________ (write) a story while her brother __________ (draw).
Answers: 1. was watching; was cooking  2. were playing; started  3. was writing; was drawing
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply the past continuous tense in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Complex Noun Phrases
grammar.explanation:
What they are
A noun phrase is a group of words that work together to describe a person, place, thing, or idea. When we add more detail — adjectives, prepositional phrases, or clauses — we create a complex noun phrase. These are very common in news writing and formal English because they allow us to pack a lot of information into a single phrase.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What they are
A noun phrase is a group of words that work together to describe a person, place, thing, or idea. When we add more detail — adjectives, prepositional phrases, or clauses — we create a complex noun phrase. These are very common in news writing and formal English because they allow us to pack a lot of information into a single phrase.
How to build one
Start with a noun: "article"
Add adjectives: "a detailed article"
Add a prepositional phrase: "a detailed article about media literacy"
Add a relative clause: "a detailed article about media literacy that was published yesterday"
More examples from news writing:
"A legally binding amendment to the school policy."
"An industrious group of delegates who worked hard to reach an agreement."
"The widely shared video claiming that the school would close."
"A well-researched investigation carried out by the student journalism club."
Common mistakes
Incorrect adjective order: "The blue big balloon." Correct: "The big blue balloon."
Using too many adjectives: "The really very big giant dog." Better: "The very large dog."
Practice
Expand each simple noun phrase into a complex noun phrase by adding at least two descriptive elements:
article → ___________
student → ___________
news report → ___________
Sample answers: 1. A well-written article about fake news.  2. An enthusiastic young student from the journalism club.  3. A breaking news report that shocked the entire community.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply complex noun phrases in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Adjective + Infinitive
grammar.explanation:
What it means
The adjective + infinitive structure combines an adjective (a describing word) with an infinitive (to + verb). It is used to express feelings, opinions, and states about actions. Common adjectives used in this pattern include necessary, happy, excited, eligible, difficult, important, and easy.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means
The adjective + infinitive structure combines an adjective (a describing word) with an infinitive (to + verb). It is used to express feelings, opinions, and states about actions. Common adjectives used in this pattern include necessary, happy, excited, eligible, difficult, important, and easy.
Structure and examples
Structure: It + is/was + adjective + to + verb
"It is necessary to consult a lawyer."
"He was eligible to vote."
"She is excited to learn new things."
"It is important to verify information before sharing it."
"It was difficult to understand the complex article."
Common mistakes
Incorrect: "It is necessary consulting a lawyer." Correct: "It is necessary to consult a lawyer."
Incorrect: "He was eligible vote." Correct: "He was eligible to vote."
Practice
Complete the sentences using the correct adjective and infinitive:
It is __________ (necessary) __________ (consult) a doctor when you feel unwell.
She is __________ (excited) __________ (visit) her grandparents.
It is __________ (important) __________ (check) news sources before sharing.
Answers: 1. necessary to consult  2. excited to visit  3. important to check
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply adjective + infinitive in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Debate: Digital Media Ethics
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
A debate is a structured discussion where two sides argue for and against a statement. Choose one of the topics below and organise a debate with a partner, a group, or your AI tutor.
Debate topics (choose one):
"Social media does more harm than good for young people."
"Schools should teach media literacy as a compulsory subject."
"People who deliberately spread fake news should face legal consequences."
"Everyone has the right to post whatever they want online, even if it is not true."
Debate rules:
Divide into two teams: For (agrees with the statement) and Against (disagrees).
Each speaker has one to two minutes to present their argument clearly.
Every speaker must use at least one opinion phrase (I think, I believe, In my opinion).
When disagreeing with the other team, begin with a polite acknowledging phrase such as "I see your point, but I think that..."
At the end, the audience votes on which team made the most convincing arguments. Discuss whether anyone changed their mind and why.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise debate: digital media ethics and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Role-Play Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
In this activity, one person plays a journalist and the other plays a person who has been involved in a news event. The journalist must ask thoughtful questions, and the interviewee must answer in character. This activity practises both questioning skills and the ability to speak fluently and expressively.
Suggested characters to interview:
Ishmael from the reading passage, after the journalism club published their article.
The headteacher of Ilyas Academy, explaining what the school has learned from the incident.
A parent who was worried by the fake video and now feels relieved.
Sarah from the story "The Duty of Truth", explaining how she stood up for fairness.
A social media expert explaining how to spot fake news.
The journalist should prepare at least five questions before the interview. After the interview, the journalist should write a short summary (3–4 sentences) using reported speech to describe what the interviewee said. For example: "Ishmael explained that the journalism club had decided to investigate the rumour because they wanted to find the truth. He added that checking sources was the most important step."
Check Your Learning
You have now worked through the whole of Unit 8. Well done! Before you move on, take a few minutes to read each statement below and think honestly about whether you can do it confidently. Place a tick next to each statement you feel sure about, and a question mark next to any that you would like to practise more. There are no wrong answers here. This is a tool to help you understand your own progress and set goals for improvement.
I can...
Explain what media literacy means and why it is important in my own words.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise role-play interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
This unit includes two writing projects. The first asks you to write a school magazine article using journalistic conventions. The second asks you to analyse a piece of fake news and explain why it is unreliable. Both projects will help you develop critical thinking, clear communication, and media literacy skills that are valuable far beyond the English classroom.
Project A — Write a School Magazine Article
Imagine your school has just launched its first student magazine. You have been chosen as one of the reporters. Your task is to write an article about a recent event at your school or in your community. The article should be between 200 and 300 words and must follow the conventions of good journalism.
Journalistic conventions to include:
A headline that is short, attention-grabbing, and accurate.
A lead paragraph (the first paragraph) that answers the five Ws: Who? What? When? Where? Why?
At least one direct quote from a person involved in the event (you may make this up if you are writing about an imaginary event).
At least one sentence using reported speech to paraphrase what someone said.
Factual, balanced language — avoid personal opinions unless you are writing an opinion column.
A concluding sentence that summarises the main point or looks ahead to what might happen next.
Suggested topics (choose one or invent your own):
A new club, team, or project that has started at your school.

activities.title: Activities 1: Mock News Broadcast
activities.instructionsAndItems:
In this activity, you will prepare and deliver a short news broadcast about an event at your school or in your community. This is an excellent way to practise clear, formal spoken English and to experience what it feels like to be a journalist presenting information to an audience.
How to prepare:
Choose a recent event to report on. It can be real or imaginary.
Write a short script (about 1 minute of speaking time, roughly 120–150 words). Include a greeting, the main news, at least one quote from a person involved, and a closing sentence.
Practise reading your script aloud at least twice. Focus on speaking clearly, at a steady pace, and with confident eye contact.
Deliver your broadcast to your family, classmates, or AI tutor. Record it if possible so you can listen back and improve.
After the broadcast, ask your audience: Was the information clear? Did the broadcaster sound confident and professional?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Debate: Digital Media Ethics
activities.instructionsAndItems:
A debate is a structured discussion where two sides argue for and against a statement. Choose one of the topics below and organise a debate with a partner, a group, or your AI tutor.
Debate topics (choose one):
"Social media does more harm than good for young people."
"Schools should teach media literacy as a compulsory subject."
"People who deliberately spread fake news should face legal consequences."
"Everyone has the right to post whatever they want online, even if it is not true."
Debate rules:
Divide into two teams: For (agrees with the statement) and Against (disagrees).
Each speaker has one to two minutes to present their argument clearly.
Every speaker must use at least one opinion phrase (I think, I believe, In my opinion).
When disagreeing with the other team, begin with a polite acknowledging phrase such as "I see your point, but I think that..."
At the end, the audience votes on which team made the most convincing arguments. Discuss whether anyone changed their mind and why.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

In this activity, one person plays a journalist and the other plays a person who has been involved in a news event. The journalist must ask thoughtful questions, and the interviewee must answer in character. This activity practises both questioning skills and the ability to speak fluently and expressively.
Suggested characters to interview:
Ishmael from the reading passage, after the journalism club published their article.
The headteacher of Ilyas Academy, explaining what the school has learned from the incident.
A parent who was worried by the fake video and now feels relieved.
Sarah from the story "The Duty of Truth", explaining how she stood up for fairness.
A social media expert explaining how to spot fake news.
The journalist should prepare at least five questions before the interview. After the interview, the journalist should write a short summary (3–4 sentences) using reported speech to describe what the interviewee said. For example: "Ishmael explained that the journalism club had decided to investigate the rumour because they wanted to find the truth. He added that checking sources was the most important step."
Check Your Learning
You have now worked through the whole of Unit 8. Well done! Before you move on, take a few minutes to read each statement below and think honestly about whether you can do it confidently. Place a tick next to each statement you feel sure about, and a question mark next to any that you would like to practise more. There are no wrong answers here. This is a tool to help you understand your own progress and set goals for improvement.
I can...
Explain what media literacy means and why it is important in my own words.

quizzes.question: What does 'express' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. | To say that something is true, but not prove it yet. | To show or tell what you think or feel.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To show or tell what you think or feel.
quizzes.explanation: I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.

quizzes.question: What does 'claim' mean?
quizzes.options: A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | The power to change or affect someone or something. | To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. | To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
quizzes.explanation: She will claim her prize after winning the contest.

quizzes.question: What does 'allege' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps different groups communicate and work together. | A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | The power to change or affect someone or something. | To say that something is true, but not prove it yet.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To say that something is true, but not prove it yet.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher alleges that the student cheated on the test.

quizzes.question: What does 'correspond' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who helps different groups communicate and work together. | The power to change or affect someone or something. | To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. | To persuade someone to believe or do something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something.
quizzes.explanation: The colours of the flags correspond with the team colours.

quizzes.question: What does 'explanation' mean?
quizzes.options: A change or addition to a law or document. | A person who helps different groups communicate and work together. | A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | To persuade someone to believe or do something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher gave a clear explanation of the problem.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Duty of Truth, part 1': "But an anonymous teacher alleges that students in Form 3 have skipped duty before." "That's a _____," Sarah said.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "But an anonymous teacher alleges that students in Form 3 have skipped duty before." "That's a claim," Sarah said.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Media, Technology, and News: source text 2': They included three pieces of evidence: a quote from the administrator, a link to the official inspection results, and an _____ of why the video was not trustworthy.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: They included three pieces of evidence: a quote from the administrator, a link to the official inspection results, and an explanation of why the video was not trustworthy.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Duty of Truth, part 1': She was the _____ between the student council and the school board — a role that required calm thinking and strong communication.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She was the liaison between the student council and the school board — a role that required calm thinking and strong communication.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Duty of Truth, part 1': "Let's write a formal letter requesting an _____ to the rule." "But who will sign it?" asked Isaac.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "Let's write a formal letter requesting an amendment to the rule." "But who will sign it?" asked Isaac.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Duty of Truth, part 1': Attendance is _____." Sarah stood reading the notice, arms folded.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Attendance is mandatory." Sarah stood reading the notice, arms folded.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model disciplinary language aloud, teach annotation and source checking explicitly, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on evidence, cohesion, viewpoint and vocabulary precision while increasing learner independence.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you explain what misinformation and
disinformation mean? What is the difference between them?'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you give me five sentences to practise
changing from direct speech to reported speech? I want ones about
news and media.'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you give me more practice with reported
speech and conditionals? I want to make sure I understand the
difference between first and second conditionals.'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you help me plan my school magazine article?
I want to write about [your chosen topic]. Can you suggest a good
headline and help me structure my lead paragraph?'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you play the role of a journalist and
interview me about a school event? I want to practise answering
questions clearly and giving detailed responses.'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you give me a quiz to test what I learned
in Unit 8 about reported speech, multi-word verbs, conditionals,
and media literacy?'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
What is the difference between 'claim' and 'allege'?
Can you help me use 'liaison' and 'correspond' in sentences about media?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain what 'liable' means in different contexts?
What does it mean to be 'eligible' for something?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
What is the difference between 'allocate' and 'delegate'?
Can you give me three more sentences using 'consult'?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me use 'hybrid' in a sentence about technology?
What does 'copious' mean? When would I use it?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you test me on my Unit 8 vocabulary?
Give me definitions and I will try to guess the word.'
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Ask your AI tutor: 'Can you check my grammar answers and explain
any mistakes? I want to make sure I understand each rule properly.'

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To show or tell what you think or feel. I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.
To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours. She will claim her prize after winning the contest.
To say that something is true, but not prove it yet. The teacher alleges that the student cheated on the test.
To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. The colours of the flags correspond with the team colours.
A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. The teacher gave a clear explanation of the problem.
The power to change or affect someone or something. Her kindness had a positive influence on her friends.
A person who helps different groups communicate and work together. The teacher acted as a liaison between the students and the principal.
To persuade someone to believe or do something. She convinced her classmates to join the journalism club.
A change or addition to a law or document. The school made an amendment to the rules to make them fairer.
Something that is required or must be done. Wearing a helmet is mandatory when riding a bike.
